comment: Ottima spiegazione… La tecnologia è affascinante anche se dividerà inevitabilmente sempre più le persone. In un futuro distopico popolato tra Eloi e di Morlock (mi riferisco al noto libro della macchina del tempo di H.G. Wells)… verso una visione socio-culturale chiamata Fandom… con AI che trovano soluzioni e risposte facili per ignoranti… una casta ristretta dov’è pochi sono sopra e molti stanno sotto i pochi… coloro che detengono il potere politico o economico… una forbice che grazie alla tecnologia dividerà abbienti e non abbienti, evoluti e capre… Fineco e Conto Banco Posta… Certo che se io avessi un figlio gli consiglierei di studiare il linguaggio del Qubit e programmare così i super computer quantistici… iper specializzarsi in qualcosa che tutti vogliono… come ad esempio sviluppare-adattare programmi Photoshop, AUTOCAD… già ottimizzati per il visore di Apple… quando uscirà questo visore (da Geek convinto), acquisterò di sicuro questo visore per sostituire l’uso del monitor ed integrarlo nel mio ecosistema Apple… Dopotutto reputo questo prodotto un significativo passo verso l’integrazione effettiva dei due mondi reale-virtuale… inoltre essendo detentore di azioni Apple, questo prodotto sicuramente otterrà i favori del mercato dei professionals… e mi ripagherà dell’investimento del visore…

video_title: Apple Vision Pro is Missing Something...
comment:
I feel like local shared experiences shouldn't be too hard.  Both devices are clearly doing some sort of SLAM like how robots do to navigate, it shouldn't be too hard to share a 3D point cloud between the two devices and sync that periodically, especially if you tag your point not just with where they are but when, then you only have to sync new data over the local wifi.

Also memory of spaces could also be done with wifi, using the network you are connected to as an identifier of "oh, I'm connected here, use this space" or even GPS if the headset has it to say "I'm at these coords, I probably need to be thinking about this point cloud".

video_title: Apple Vision Pro Review: Tomorrow's Ideas... Today's Tech!
comment:
I think look-based UX is just as bad as it's always been. Meta's UX for controllerless input, while it is admittedly a bit floaty in its current form and has some trouble deciding what angle to draw the ray from, it is in concept significantly more friendly. There is in fact a reason why we've not all tossed out our mice for Tobi eye trackers. A pointing method divorced from your look position is just nicer to use.

And I'm sure with how much high end tech they crammed into this device, their implementation of hands as pointers would have been just as smooth as their position tracking, and way more reliable than meta's version. Keep in mind, this thing costs 8 times as much as a quest 3, so that's not a dig at meta, apple just has more data to work with due to having more and higher end sensors. As alluded to though, apple does have a stick up their butt about reinventing wheels all the time, so this is likely different for the sake of different, even though this is only different from other look based UX in that you pinch fingers to click instead of staring long enough for the circle to fill up. This is an upgrade on that for sure, but if you've got sophisticated hand tracking already, why only use it to simulate a mouse button and scroll wheel?

I think this was a mistake, and a missed opportunity to have the best controller-free UX on the market. Instead we have two unideal UXes; meta's, which is more natural but suffers from having not quite accurate enough tracking, and apple's which has high accuracy tracking, but only lets you point with your eyeballs.

For what it's worth, eye tracking is also significantly easier to do, and the quest three has their version of look based UX (the old one where you have to fill a circle to click) but it would take little effort to add a look+pinch mode that would be pretty much identical to apples UX. Which is just to say, it seems really odd that they committed so hard to "no controllers", had expensive high end hardware, but then went with a UX that feels like a downgrade from the $500 headset's UX.

comment:
I feel like I would be about 1000% more interested in these types of things if they JUST had an OPTIONAL, if they must, little tiny finger-ring style controller with a SINGLE button, at least! I'd MUCH MUCH MUCH rather click a little fidget-spinner like click rather than feel like a tool clacking my fingers together. I did this in VR w/ the Oculus and it was OK but felt bad in every way. However, design wise you get SO much more space just adding binary yes-no checking, which is what the tapping together does. Like it feels somewhat crampy, seems strange to do with interoception, looks odd from the outside which, even if no one is around, takes me out of the experience. Doing a button is something we ALL have accepted as normal hardware usage and does not take me out of anything at all after decades of game controller use. Plus you can rest your hands easier. 

They have these tiny, 1inch x 1inch tiktok scrollers with like a D-pad and 2-5 buttons on it. THAT is what I want. THEN I might consider AR.

video_title: Celebrating and questioning the Apple Vision Pro after one year, on the AppleInsider Podcast
comment:
Ich bin der Meinung dass es nicht so einfach “zu trennen“ ist. Wobei ich wirtschaftlich abwägen würde ob ich auf einen Tesla verzichten kann. Wenn ich vor der Entscheidung stünde ein neues Elektroauto zu kaufen und es eine wirtschaftlich sinnvolle Alternative zu einem Tesla gäbe, würde ich zur Alternative greifen.
Tesla und Elon sind einfach stärker verknüpft als andere Unternehmen mit deren CEO. Es war schon lange so dass Elon durch sein Verhalten, seine Tweets, Interviews einen sehr großen Teil zum Marketing beigetragen hat. Genau deshalb ist es auch ok eben dies in der Kaufentscheidung zu berücksichtigen.

Ich würde aber andersherum niemanden Verurteilen der sagt „ich trenne das“. Wenn ich seit Jahren mit den Produkten zufrieden war oder mein Geld mit Tesla verdiene ist das vollkommen ok diese Produkte weiter zu kaufen bzw damit Geschäfte zu machen.

video_title: Celebrating and questioning the Apple Vision Pro after one year, on the AppleInsider Podcast
comment: Have had mine for just shy of a year now.  Use it hours everyday for most everything I would use any of my other devises for.  My Apple TV, phone, iPad, MacBook.  Just watched Monster Hunter in 3D and I can’t stress enough while watching it, just how weird it is knowing that this incredible experience exists in the world but no one else I know has any idea. My girlfriend has bad vision and can’t experience it.  It seriously just feels so weird that something so incredible to experience is still so relatively unknown.  Really hoping the cost improves enough that more people can enjoy it.

video_title: Celebrating and questioning the Apple Vision Pro after one year, on the AppleInsider Podcast
comment: Way too expensive, especially in Canada. I went into the store and was blown away. Even with only one good eye. Perhaps worth it for those with deep pockets but unattainable to those who live in the real world. It could be revolutionary but needs to be much cheaper, lighter and completely compatible with all gaming platforms. What will happen to Apple and all of its products when Trump tariffs China?

comment:
I feel like local shared experiences shouldn't be too hard.  Both devices are clearly doing some sort of SLAM like how robots do to navigate, it shouldn't be too hard to share a 3D point cloud between the two devices and sync that periodically, especially if you tag your point not just with where they are but when, then you only have to sync new data over the local wifi.

Also memory of spaces could also be done with wifi, using the network you are connected to as an identifier of "oh, I'm connected here, use this space" or even GPS if the headset has it to say "I'm at these coords, I probably need to be thinking about this point cloud".

comment:
I got my AVP on day 1 and love it. It has many 1st gen limitations and it is bulky and expensive. But I’m old enough to remember Mac OS X 10.0, the 1st gen iMac, iPod, iPhone, iTunes, AppleTV, Apple Watch, AirPods, etc. They were all mocked for one reason or another. AVP’s drawbacks aren’t fatal flaws or insurmountable obstacles for the future. 

People will slowly turn on to it as the weight and price come down, apps get developed and optimized, and more content gets pushed out. eventually I would love to see eye and hand tracking cross-pollinate to other platforms too. The upcoming software changes to iOS 19 and macOS could be hints at what’s to come.

video_title: Vision Pro has an all-new Guest Mode!

We got one for work and someone logged on with their personal account, it synced all there photos and I could not log them off.  Finally when the came back from holiday they logged off, but there photos were all synced to the AVP.
Nothing too dodgy on there, but worrying on a shared headset.
I need to look into the guest mode, But don’t want to lock it down for anyone.
I guess there is compromise to how we plan to use it in work.
One main User and guests, or open access.

 It is better than quest visual quality, but the quest link to steam, and pc is more useful than locked in apple apps.
Hopefully more apps for pc linking will come out in July.

comment: Most people seem to be confused as to why Apply would release a brand new product that is more expensive than what most people can afford. Which is a fair mistake to make... Apple is so huge that it can now afford to gamble on a revolutionary product category and as opposed to internal research and development, they have enough of a strong brand that they can put out expensive items that fewer people will buy so that developers can get ahold of it. So that people can review it. So that they can recieve a metric ton of information from people and small tech companies alike on how to streamline and mass produce the future models that will then be more affordable. THis is actually a smart move by Apple in my opinion on such a risky product. They don't want everyone to own a half-baked or less than optimal product seeing as how they aren't the actual inventors of AR or VR. So they are testing the waters. And they will learn from this and make the future best ever AR headset in the world, hopefully.

Trying Apple Vision Pro - What's it actually like? Q&A
comment: It feels like it's 2007 again and the 499$ 4GB iPhone is here. Everyone laughed at the price, sales were underwhelming in the first few months, people had mixed feelings, and no one knew the extent to which it would go and completely change the entire tech industry and all of society. Like with the iPhone, the price of the Vision Pro will go down significantly with the second and third generations. It still won't be cheap but it will definitely be more accessible which is ultimately the point. Presumably they'll also make a non-pro version that would cost about as much as a Pro model iPhone at around 1200-1500$ to lure more users into this new spacial OS ecosystem. All of a sudden, people who previously could not afford full desktop setups with multiple monitors or large TVs can have that experience with this one device and people who already physically have such setups and TVs will enjoy the freedom of being able to take those with them and use them anywhere. The same way everyone has smartphones, eventually everyone will have an Apple Vision or Apple Vision Pro in around 10 years or so. What's important to consider is that even if this product is widely adopted and successful, it will NOT replace smartphones and computers. When smartphones were still a relatively new thing, lots of people still carried around dedicated cameras and iPods for many years before they became obsolete and/or unnecessary. What WILL replace smartphones and computers is a Vision Pro that's as compact and light as eyeglasses and maybe one day even contact lenses or some variation of Neural Link in the form of a direct brain implant. Though such tech is still decades away, we could potentially see Apple Vision Pro glasses sometime between 2035 and 2040, so smartphones and computers as we know them will most likely still be around for many more years to come. We are definitely in for a very exciting future and it will be quite interesting to see companies like Samsung and Google competing with Apple in this new market. Perhaps even a surprise new company? As a final remark, considering how AI is going to significantly improve smartphones, starting with the Galaxy S24 series and either the iPhone 16 or 17, imagine how powerful an AI supercharged Apple Vision Pro would be. The tech plateau is finally going uphill again!

comment: Even as someone who's super into VR and hopes it'll reach Ready Player One levels of tech one day, I absolutely despise the AVP. The fact that you can't even customize your "persona" to make it look exactly how you want is the ultimate dealbreaker for me. It just pisses me off how it HAS to be based on your real face. Seriously, I'm ugly asf and wanna be able to escape that by using VR. The Quest 3 on the other hand, allows you to choose literally everything about how your avatar looks. You can be as hot and pretty of a girl as you want, which is the biggest reason I even use the Quest in the first place, ngl. While I do feel like the Meta avatars are a bit too cartoonish, I'll still take them over the restrictive personas on the AVP anyday. Until Apple gives us full customization over our avatar appearances like Meta, I will never, EVER put my hands anywhere near their pos headset

video_title: Using Apple Vision Pro: What It’s Actually Like!
comment:
I feel like I would be about 1000% more interested in these types of things if they JUST had an OPTIONAL, if they must, little tiny finger-ring style controller with a SINGLE button, at least! I'd MUCH MUCH MUCH rather click a little fidget-spinner like click rather than feel like a tool clacking my fingers together. I did this in VR w/ the Oculus and it was OK but felt bad in every way. However, design wise you get SO much more space just adding binary yes-no checking, which is what the tapping together does. Like it feels somewhat crampy, seems strange to do with interoception, looks odd from the outside which, even if no one is around, takes me out of the experience. Doing a button is something we ALL have accepted as normal hardware usage and does not take me out of anything at all after decades of game controller use. Plus you can rest your hands easier. 

They have these tiny, 1inch x 1inch tiktok scrollers with like a D-pad and 2-5 buttons on it. THAT is what I want. THEN I might consider AR.

Wenn ich vor der Entscheidung stünde ein neues Elektroauto zu kaufen und es eine wirtschaftlich sinnvolle Alternative zu einem Tesla gäbe, würde ich zur Alternative greifen.
Tesla und Elon sind einfach stärker verknüpft als andere Unternehmen mit deren CEO. Es war schon lange so dass Elon durch sein Verhalten, seine Tweets, Interviews einen sehr großen Teil zum Marketing beigetragen hat. Genau deshalb ist es auch ok eben dies in der Kaufentscheidung zu berücksichtigen.

Ich würde aber andersherum niemanden Verurteilen der sagt „ich trenne das“. Wenn ich seit Jahren mit den Produkten zufrieden war oder mein Geld mit Tesla verdiene ist das vollkommen ok diese Produkte weiter zu kaufen bzw damit Geschäfte zu machen.

comment:
Did the demonstration at our local Apple store.  Very impressive.  Less than $aud 7k, including two sets of optics for myself and my wife.  We are both very old Australians. Retired to the Australian Capital Territory.  
Because my body is severely disabled with Traumatic Brain Injury, 40 years ago, if could be adjusted OK, to only using my one working hand, my right hand and thumb and first finger. 

The included virtual keyboard had word anticipations, but will it have swipe text input, grammar and multi language options? 
Apple does not have the thousands of user options of the other most popular computer systems. 
Two sets of batteries, gives 3 hours, plus 3 hours, of continuous viewing, such as long travel journeys, night time or hospital residents.  
The unit is heavy on the head, as mentioned in the YouTube review here.  So the third party accessorise will probably be needed. 
We could watch standard tv, videos, and internet sites. Unsure of the cost and availably of media material that might exist after the first few months of ownership. 
Impressed that we did not need the other Apple products.  We are Microsoft Windows and Android focused. 
The Safari Internet browser allows usual integer links to work. Netflix. YouTube. Google, etc. Zoom and other VoIP applications might audio work? 
It might also work with home automation. Answering the door.  Controlling the lights, curtains, etc. 

After working as an Accreditation Surveyor in our health industries, it could be used to train users at staff with new procedures, machines, techniques and processes. Apple is famous for not cooperating with international standards.  Hardware, software, operating systems, ISO, IEEE, Windows, Android and open source productions. 
Perhaps the thousands of independent creators behind the dominant systems (Microsoft, Google and Linux) might arrive soon with a more internationally itself tool.

video_title: How Vision Pro Can BOOST Your Productivity!
comment:
foveated rendering uses hardware eye tracking in combination with software to allocate GPU power to wherever you’re looking on the display and make it as high fidelity as possible, and then everything else outside of your center vision is lower fidelity. this circle of clarity follows wherever you are looking on the display.

The reason why the edges of the DISPLAY are not very sharp has nothing to do with foveated rendering. it’s simply a hardware limitation due to lensing and probably other physical factors that no one has figured out yet how to overcome in this current form factor.

video_title: How Vision Pro Can BOOST Your Productivity!
comment:
I think you are likely confusing foveated rendering with just the fact that things that are near the edges of the field of view are blurrier because of the optics.
Foveated rendering is based on where you eyes are looking - so if you are looking down at the bottom of a document, it is rendering that as high quality as it can. But if then moving you head to "recenter" it makes it sharper - that is an optics limitation, not foveated rendering. While the end result is the same, foveated rendering shouldn't get the blame for that :)

video_title: Experience Spatial Computing NOW With Xreal Air AR Glasses!
comment:
As a skeptic of the technology, I first purchased a pair of cheaper TCL glasses (on sale )about a year ago to test drive a version of this type of tech. Long story short, the tech is impressive but a novelty, especially with the TCL glasses, as they are heavier, uncomfortable, and did not have any way to adjust brightness on-device. With the pending release of XReal Beam, I decided to give the XReal Airs a shot. Out of the box, no Nebula software at all, these glasses stand head and shoulders above my older TCL glasses, almost reinventing the experience altogether. The Airs are unexpectedly light (even if you expect them to be light) and extremely comfortable to wear for long periods. Content really pops and looks amazing on these things, giving you a new and innovative way to experience what otherwise might be same-old, same-old. Out of the box, this feels like the best pair of glasses you can get for a personal display. 

The Nebula app—the beta software unlocking spatial computing capability—is a fun curiosity at this point, working reasonably well for what it is, but has its own limitations that the pending Vision Pro probably will not have. For example, the resolution of the virtual desktops is extremely low, on my 14” MacBook Pro they were around 720p each while the main display remained 1920x1080. I use an open source app called Retina DisplayMenu to adjust my screen resolution (typically higher than that for my young eagle eyes), so in some ways this doesn’t really increase my productivity (especially considering how easy it is to simply just use Spaces instead, maintaining whatever screen resolution I want). Put another way, you may be given up screen real estate for your AR experience, something I haven’t heard any reviewer really mention. That said, it’s still neat-o to experience, but I wouldn’t rush out and buy these glasses just for the spatial computing component nor would I presume these will match a forthcoming device to cost 10x more.

video_title: Apple Vision Pro visionOS: Hands On with the UI, Apps, & More!
comment:
Instead of only viewing a window upright in front of us, I'm hoping we can view windows and other digital elements on a flat surface like a desk - like paper. Imagine viewing a multi-page PDF on your desk like a real paper document. You could make it larger than a real paper document, zoom in, and scroll or flip through the pages like a real document. Maybe even have a slight shadow underneath it depending on the light in your environment. Imagine designing a poster using Adobe Illustrator or Photoshop with the Apple Pencil becoming a real art tool like a paint brush. You could work on your art either upright or flat on a desk or floor. You could move the Adobe panels/palettes around your desk and they would be three-dimensional like real art tools. At this point, viewing everything inside a floating window just seems a bit uninspired. It's cool, but I think we want dimensional objects we can "hold." Also, imagine a sculpture app that allows you to create virtual pottery on a virtual pottery wheel with your hands, or use your ApplePencil as a chisel to carve a life-sized sculpture of Michelangelo's David from a virtual block of marble.

I don't know how I feel about the apps being circular instead of the usual squares with rounded corners. If they kept them as squares, it would tie in with the other OSs for consistency. The circles crop too much into the app's art. It works well for Safari and Photos because the icons are circular. But it feels tight when you see the multiple apps grouped into that one circle. They might be trying to copy the app icons on the AppleWatch. I'm excited to see where this goes.

video_title: Does visionOS 2 Make Vision Pro WORTH It?!
comment:
Top of my wishlist would be an alternative selection gesture — an alternative to the thumb-index-finger pinch.  Not suggesting that the pinch is difficult to do, but exactly the opposite:  It’s too easy to do unintentionally/unknowingly do!  

I’ve seen a lot of cases where I’m trying like crazy to select something, and it just won’t select.  Why?  Because, unbeknownst to me, my _other hand_ has fallen into my lap with my forefinger touching my thumb!  Yes, I know we can limit pinches to one hand, but if you do so, you can no longer size things with a two-handed pinch.  Plus, I still sometimes inadvertently pinch with the one hand.

An alternative gesture thumb-to-second finger would be very helpful.

video_title: Does visionOS 2 Make Vision Pro WORTH It?!
comment:
- I can imagine air traffic controllers and the military will find the Apple Vision Pro useful to show many screens simultaneously that are 150-400 inches each...that's a lot of information.
- Current F16 figher helmets are $400k each with just a HUD, whereas the AVP could be incorporated into a helmet for far less than $400k.
- Infantry soldiers wearing a helmet with the AVP.
- AVP's passthrough is 120 degrees which allow real world interaction with the firearm, target, throwing/catching.
- I want to get the Apple Vision Pro, it's not that expensive compared to a great home theater, and people don't realize that it's got eye tracking (no mouse cursor), an 8k screen, many many 300 in screens, 3D camera, spatial audio speakers, creates an Imax or the biggest commercial theater screen size even when you're sitting in a tight coach airline seat.
- the eye tracking (replaced a cursor) us a game changer.
- way easier to develop than Facebook Quest.
- AVP is not constrained to a box game model like the Quest, instead the AVP world model is infinite.
- air traffic controllers can see the perspective of a commercial plane pilot landing.
- The AVP's many many virtual 150in-300in screens can replace a commercial airline cockpit's many physical screens, and that physical space can hold more important physical equipments.

video_title: Apple Vision Pro VS Meta Quest 3! HONEST Comparison!
comment: I'm a huge Apple fanboy and this video even made me cringe. Having used the meta quest 3 for about 3 months now and starting to use the Apple Vision Pro more, there is no comparison. The Apple Vision Pro has better build quality, better pass through and hardware, but that's where it all falls apart. I think Apple wants you to spend $3500 for those features and nothing else. I can't believe I'm saying this because I hate Facebook and Meta with a passion, the Meta Quest 3 is the way better product over all. Sure its build is not metal and glass, but it also weights a lot less which is way more important. Then the software, this is the deal breaker for me when it comes to the Vision Pro, you can run iPad apps and a hand full of VR apps for $3500. The software library on the MQ3 is so much better and with the lack of controllers and tracking shooters and games like that are going to be so much harder to build for the Vision Pro.
